source_label: Bitcoin Optech — Topics on Lightning Network
excerpt: Private channels are not announced via gossip. Senders learn about them through route hints in the receiver's invoice — the receiver effectively says 'here are the unannounced channels that lead to me.' This trades discoverability for privacy and is the standard configuration for end-user wallets that aren't trying to earn routing fees.
body:
Source excerpt:
Private channels are not announced via gossip. Senders learn about them through route hints in the receiver's invoice — the receiver effectively says 'here are the unannounced channels that lead to me.' This trades discoverability for privacy and is the standard configuration for end-user wallets that aren't trying to earn routing fees.

---

Cited by leaf "BOLT-7 gossip and the channel graph":
For routing to work, every Lightning node needs a reasonably fresh view of the public channel graph. BOLT-7 specifies the *gossip protocol* that propagates this information without requiring a central directory. Three message types do the work: `channel_announcement` advertises a new public channel (signed by both endpoint nodes and both funding-output keys, which proves the channel actually exists on the Bitcoin chain), `channel_update` advertises the current routing policy (fee rates, minimum and maximum HTLC sizes, `cltv_expiry_delta`), and `node_announcement` carries node metadata like the alias, colour, and the addresses on which the node accepts connections [1].

Channel announcements are anchored to the on-chain funding transaction. The announcing nodes provide the `short_channel_id` (block height, transaction index, output index), and any peer can verify that the named UTXO is in fact a 2-of-2 multisig of the announced keys. This is what stops a node from announcing fake channels — every claimed channel is backed by real, verifiable on-chain capital, and adding a fake announcement requires committing real bitcoin to a real funding transaction. When the funding output is later spent (channel close), the channel is implicitly removed from the graph by every node that observes the chain [2].

`channel_update` is the part that changes most often. A node typically re-broadcasts updates when it changes its fee policy or when its peer goes offline (signaling that the channel is currently unroutable in that direction). To prevent gossip spam, BOLT-7 enforces rate limits, stale-update suppression, and the requirement that updates be strictly newer than the last one accepted for the same channel/direction. Initial sync uses range queries (`query_channel_range` / `query_short_channel_ids`) so a fresh node can backfill the graph efficiently from one or two peers instead of waiting for the full broadcast cycle.

There is a fundamental tension in the design. More gossip means better path-finding and higher routing reliability, but it also leaks information about the network's topology and (indirectly) about which channels are seeing high traffic. *Private channels* — channels that are never announced via gossip — are routed to via *route hints* embedded in invoices: the receiver tells the sender, in the invoice, the specific channels that lead to them, signed but not broadcast publicly. Routing nodes that operate at scale typically have a mix of announced public channels (for inbound forwarding fees) and private channels (for direct settlement with major counterparties).

The gossip protocol is what makes the network discoverable but is also the part most under iteration. BOLT-7 v2 work and the proposed taproot-aware `channel_announcement_2` aim to reduce both the on-chain footprint per channel (by eliminating the need to publish both funding-output keys) and the bandwidth per gossip message. These changes also open the door to channels backed by something other than a 2-of-2 multisig — a meaningful simplification that has been blocked for years on coordination across implementations.

---

Cited by leaf "Payment channels and the funding transaction":
A Lightning payment channel is a 2-of-2 multisig output on the Bitcoin chain that two parties agree to treat as a private ledger between themselves. The channel is opened with a single on-chain *funding transaction* that locks bitcoin into the multisig; from that point onward the parties can update their balance allocation thousands of times by exchanging signed off-chain *commitment transactions*, none of which touch the Bitcoin chain unless the channel is closed [1].

Each commitment transaction is asymmetric. My version of the current state pays you immediately and pays me on a relative timelock (CSV); your version of the same state pays me immediately and pays you on a timelock. Whenever the parties agree on a new state, they exchange the per-commitment *revocation secret* for the previous state. If you ever broadcast an old commitment, I can use the revocation secret to sweep both your output and mine before your timelock expires — this is the *justice transaction* that keeps both parties honest without a third-party arbiter [2].

The happy path is a *cooperative close*: the parties agree on the final state and exchange a single mutually-signed transaction paying out the current balances. One on-chain transaction, no timelocks, both outputs spendable immediately. The unhappy path is a *force close*, used when one party is offline or uncooperative: the other broadcasts the most recent commitment unilaterally and waits out the `to_self_delay` window (typically 144 to 1008 blocks) before being able to spend its own output. HTLC outputs in flight at force-close time have their own cleanup paths via HTLC-timeout and HTLC-success transactions, each with its own timelock [3].

The funding transaction is what gives Lightning its security model. The worst case for any participant is that they end up settling on Bitcoin's chain with the most recent state they can prove. They never need to trust the counterparty with custody — only with availability long enough to exchange a final commitment, plus a mechanism (a watchtower, or simply being online) to punish breach attempts.

Channels can be unidirectional, bidirectional with explicit liquidity in both directions, or part of a routed payment graph where the channel's role is to forward payments for fees rather than to settle balance between the two endpoints. The economics of channel sizing — how much capital to commit, in which direction, and for how long — is the operational question that separates a routing node that earns fees from one whose capital sits idle.
